title: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992
chapters: Chapter 52 (1992) · Royal Assent 16 July 1992 · 303 Sections + Schedules · Consolidated from Trade Union and Labour Relations Act 1974, Employment Acts 1980/1982/1988/1990, Trade Union Act 1984, and others · Extensively amended by Employment Relations Acts 1999/2004, Trade Union Act 2016, and Employment Rights Act 2025
desc: The Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 is the principal statute governing collective labour relations in Great Britain. It defines trade unions and their legal status, protects the right of workers to organise into or leave a union without suffering discrimination or detriment, provides the framework for collective bargaining between unions and employers, regulates industrial action (including ballot requirements, notice periods, and the statutory immunity), and establishes collective redundancy consultation obligations. For employers, TULRCA creates significant compliance exposure across multiple areas: the duty to collectively consult on redundancies (s.188, up to 90 days' protective award per employee for failure), protection of employees from dismissal or detriment for trade union membership or activities (automatically unfair dismissal), the prohibition on inducements to bypass collective bargaining (s.145B, mandatory fixed award per worker per offer — Kostal v Dunkley [2021] UKSC 47), and the ACAS Code of Practice framework (s.199, s.207A — 25% award adjustment). The Employment Rights Act 2025 extensively amends TULRCA's industrial action provisions — simplified ballots, extended mandates, removal of 12-week protected period, reduced notice periods. This file covers the key employer-facing provisions; the full Act is available at legislation.gov.uk.

sections.title: Duty of employer to consult representatives
sections.summary: Where an employer proposes to dismiss as redundant 20 or more employees at one establishment within a period of 90 days or less, the employer must consult appropriate representatives of the affected employees. Consultation must begin at least 30 days before the first dismissal takes effect (for 20–99 employees) or at least 45 days (for 100+ employees). Consultation must be about ways of avoiding the dismissals, reducing the numbers to be dismissed, and mitigating the consequences. The consultation must be undertaken with a view to reaching agreement. The employer must disclose in writing: the reasons for the proposals, the numbers and descriptions of employees proposed for dismissal, the total number of employees of that description at the establishment, the proposed method of selecting employees, the proposed method of carrying out the dismissals including the period over which they are to take effect, and the proposed method of calculating any redundancy payments. If there are special circumstances making it not reasonably practicable to comply, the employer must take all such steps as are reasonably practicable. The Employment Rights Act 2025 proposes to remove the 'at one establishment' limitation — meaning the threshold would apply across the entire business.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer is proposing to dismiss as redundant 20 or more employees at one establishment within a period of 90 days or less, the employer shall consult about the dismissals all the persons who are appropriate representatives of any of the employees who may be affected by the proposed dismissals or may be affected by measures taken in connection with those dismissals.

(1A) The consultation shall begin in good time and in any event—
(a) where the employer is proposing to dismiss 100 or more employees as mentioned in subsection (1), at least 45 days, and
(b) otherwise, at least 30 days,
before the first of the dismissals takes effect.

(2) The consultation shall include consultation about ways of—
(a) avoiding the dismissals,
(b) reducing the numbers of employees to be dismissed, and
(c) mitigating the consequences of the dismissals,
and shall be undertaken by the employer with a view to reaching agreement with the appropriate representatives.

(4) For the purposes of the consultation the employer shall disclose in writing to the appropriate representatives—
(a) the reasons for his proposals,
(b) the numbers and descriptions of employees whom it is proposed to dismiss as redundant,
(c) the total number of employees of any such description employed by the employer at the establishment in question,
(d) the proposed method of selecting the employees who may be dismissed,
(e) the proposed method of carrying out the dismissals, with due regard to any agreed procedure, including the period over which the dismissals are to take effect, and
(f) the proposed method of calculating the amount of any redundancy payments to be made to employees who may be dismissed.

(7) If in any case there are special circumstances which render it not reasonably practicable for the employer to comply with a requirement of subsection (1A), (2) or (4), the employer shall take all such steps towards compliance with that requirement as are reasonably practicable in those circumstances.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision. Required 90 days' consultation for 100+ redundancies and 30 days for 20–99.
Consultation period for 100+ redundancies reduced from 90 days to 45 days by Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (Amendment) Order 2013.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 proposes to remove the 'at one establishment' limitation in s.188(1). The 20-employee threshold would apply across the entire business, not per establishment. This overrides the interpretation in USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd [2015] ICR 675 (CA). For multi-site employers, redundancies across all locations would be aggregated for consultation threshold purposes.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Enacted. Not yet in force. Subject to commencement orders.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: Dismissal Procedures category. Transformational for multi-site employers. FM sector employers with depot-based operations (e.g. Northerly Hill: Cardiff, Swansea, Bristol, Birmingham) would aggregate all depot redundancies for the threshold calculation.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 proposes to remove the 'at one establishment' limitation. This would overturn USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd [2015] and significantly expand collective consultation obligations for multi-site employers. Forward Intelligence classification: HIGH (Royal Assent achieved, commencement expected October 2026).

leadingCases.name: USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd (in administration) [2015] ICR 675
leadingCases.court: Court of Appeal (following ECJ reference in Lyttle, Case C-392/13)
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that 'establishment' in s.188(1) means the local employment unit to which the redundant workers are assigned, not the entire undertaking. This limits collective consultation obligations for multi-site employers — redundancies at each site are counted separately for the 20-employee threshold.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on meaning of 'establishment' — ERA 2025 proposes to override this interpretation by removing the establishment limitation entirely

leadingCases.name: Junk v Kühnel [2005] ECJ Case C-188/03
leadingCases.heldText: The ECJ held that 'proposing to dismiss' means when the employer is contemplating or planning collective redundancies — consultation must begin before any final decision is made. Consultation must begin while the employer's proposals are still at a formative stage.
leadingCases.significance: Established that consultation must occur at the formative stage of proposals, before final decisions are taken

leadingCases.name: Hardy v Tourism South East [2005] IRLR 242
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the duty to consult is triggered when the employer proposes to dismiss — this means when the employer has reached a stage where redundancy dismissals are genuinely proposed, even if the identity of individuals has not yet been determined.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the trigger point for collective consultation obligations

refs: s.189 TULRCA 1992 (complaint and protective award — up to 90 days' pay per employee)
s.193 TULRCA 1992 (duty to notify Secretary of State — HR1 form)
ERA 1996 s.139 (definition of redundancy)
Collective Redundancies Directive 98/59/EC
Employment Rights Act 2025 (proposed removal of 'at one establishment')
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/188

sections.title: Complaint and protective award
sections.summary: Where an employer fails to comply with the duty to consult under s.188, a complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal by the appropriate representatives, a trade union, or an affected employee. If the tribunal finds the complaint well-founded, it must make a declaration to that effect and may make a protective award — an order that the employer pay remuneration for a protected period to employees who have been or may be dismissed as redundant. The protected period is such period as the tribunal determines to be just and equitable, up to a maximum of 90 days beginning with the date on which the first dismissal takes effect. If the employer fails to pay, the individual employee may enforce the award. The protective award is a penalty for the employer's failure to consult, not compensation for the employee's loss — it is payable even if the employee has found alternative employment.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employer has failed to comply with a requirement of section 188 or section 188A, a complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal on that ground—
(a) in the case of a failure relating to the election of employee representatives, by any of the affected employees or by any of the employees who have been or may be dismissed as redundant;
(b) in the case of any other failure relating to employee representatives, by any of the employee representatives to whom the failure related;
(c) in the case of failure relating to representatives of a trade union, by the trade union; and
(d) in any other case, by any of the affected employees or by any of the employees who have been or may be dismissed as redundant.

(2) If the tribunal finds the complaint well-founded it shall make a declaration to that effect and may also make a protective award.

(3) A protective award is an award in respect of one or more descriptions of employees—
(a) who have been dismissed as redundant, or whom it is proposed to dismiss as redundant, and
(b) in respect of whose dismissal or proposed dismissal the employer has failed to comply with a requirement of section 188,
ordering the employer to pay remuneration for the protected period.

(4) The protected period—
(a) begins with the date on which the first of the dismissals to which the complaint relates takes effect, or the date of the award, whichever is the earlier, and
(b) is of such length as the tribunal determines to be just and equitable in all the circumstances having regard to the seriousness of the employer's default.

(4B) The protected period shall not exceed 90 days.

versions.description: Original provision. Maximum protected period aligned with the pre-2013 90-day consultation period for 100+ redundancies.
Employment Rights Act 2025 doubles the maximum protective award period. Not yet in force — subject to commencement order (expected April 2026).

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 doubles the maximum protective award period from 90 days to 180 days. This significantly increases the financial exposure for employers who fail to collectively consult. For a workforce of 100 employees at average UK salary, the maximum exposure increases from approximately £1.5M to £3M.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: Dismissal Procedures category. Doubles maximum financial exposure for collective consultation failures.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 doubles the maximum protective award from 90 to 180 days. Forward Intelligence classification: CONFIRMED (in Commencement No. 2 Regulations, effective 6 April 2026).

leadingCases.name: Susie Radin Ltd v GMB [2004] EWCA Civ 180
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the starting point for the protective award should be the maximum period (then 90 days) and it should be reduced only if there are mitigating circumstances. The award is penal in nature — it punishes the employer for failing to consult, not to compensate employees for their loss.
leadingCases.significance: Established that the maximum protective award is the starting point — reduction only for mitigating circumstances

refs: s.188 TULRCA 1992 (duty to consult)
s.190–192 TULRCA 1992 (entitlement under protective award, complaint by employee)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/189

sections.title: Meaning of collective agreement and collective bargaining
sections.summary: A collective agreement is any agreement or arrangement made by or on behalf of one or more trade unions and one or more employers or employers' associations and relating to one or more of the specified matters. Collective bargaining means negotiations relating to those matters. The specified matters include: terms and conditions of employment, engagement or non-engagement or termination or suspension of workers, allocation of work, discipline, trade union membership or facilities, negotiating machinery, and any other matter falling within the agreement.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision from TULRA 1974 and related legislation. Definition of collective agreement and collective bargaining unchanged since consolidation.

leadingCases.name: Keeley v Fosroc International Ltd [2006] EWCA Civ 1277
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that terms of a collective agreement can be incorporated into individual contracts of employment, becoming legally enforceable at individual level even though the collective agreement itself is not enforceable between the union and employer.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on incorporation of collective agreement terms into individual contracts.

refs: s.179 TULRCA 1992 (collective agreements presumed not legally enforceable)
s.181 TULRCA 1992 (employer's duty to disclose information for collective bargaining)
Schedule A1 TULRCA 1992 (statutory recognition procedure)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/178

sections.title: Whether collective agreement intended to be a legally enforceable contract
sections.summary: A collective agreement is conclusively presumed not to have been intended by the parties to be a legally enforceable contract unless the agreement is in writing and contains a provision which (however expressed) states that the parties intend that the agreement shall be a legally enforceable contract. This reflects the UK tradition of legal abstentionism from collective bargaining — collective agreements set the framework for employment terms but are not directly enforceable as contracts between the union and the employer. However, terms agreed collectively may be incorporated into individual employment contracts and become enforceable at individual level.
sections.text:
(1) A collective agreement shall be conclusively presumed not to have been intended by the parties to be a legally enforceable contract unless the agreement—
(a) is in writing, and
(b) contains a provision which (however expressed) states that the parties intend that the agreement shall be a legally enforceable contract.

(2) A collective agreement which does satisfy those conditions shall be conclusively presumed to have been intended by the parties to be a legally enforceable contract.

(3) If a collective agreement is in writing and contains a provision which (however expressed) states that the parties intend that one or more parts of the agreement specified in that provision, but not the whole of the agreement, shall be a legally enforceable contract, then—
(a) the specified part or parts of the agreement shall be conclusively presumed to have been intended by the parties to be a legally enforceable contract, and
(b) the remainder of the agreement shall be conclusively presumed not to have been intended by the parties to be such a contract.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision. The presumption against enforceability of collective agreements dates from TULRA 1974 s.18, itself re-enacting the position established by the Industrial Relations Act 1971 (reversed) and then TULRA 1974.

leadingCases.name: National Coal Board v National Union of Mineworkers [1986] ICR 736
leadingCases.heldText: Scott J held that a collective agreement about colliery review procedures was not a legally enforceable contract in the absence of express provision to the contrary. The presumption against enforceability is conclusive and can only be rebutted by a written provision expressly stating the agreement is intended to be legally enforceable.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the conclusive nature of the presumption against enforceability of collective agreements

refs: s.178 TULRCA 1992 (definition of collective agreement)
ERA 1996 s.1(4)(j) (written statement must refer to applicable collective agreements)
Keeley v Fosroc International Ltd [2006] EWCA Civ 1277 (incorporation of collective terms into individual contracts)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/179

parts.title: Protection of Workers — Trade Union Membership and Activities (ss.137–167)

sections.title: Inducements relating to collective bargaining
sections.summary: A worker has the right not to have an offer made to them by the employer where the sole or main purpose of the offer is to achieve the result that one or more of the worker's terms of employment will not (or will no longer) be determined by collective agreement negotiated by or on behalf of the trade union. Where a tribunal finds the complaint well-founded, it must make an award of a fixed statutory sum to the worker for each offer (currently £5,641, reviewed annually). This is a mandatory fixed award — the tribunal has no discretion over the amount. In Kostal UK Ltd v Dunkley [2021], the Supreme Court confirmed that making direct pay offers to employees during ongoing collective bargaining can constitute a breach of s.145B. For large workforces, liability escalates rapidly — each individual offer to each worker triggers a separate award.
sections.text:
(1) A worker who is a member of an independent trade union which is recognised, or seeking to be recognised, by his employer has the right not to have an offer made to him by his employer if—
(a) acceptance of the offer, together with other workers' acceptance of offers which the employer also makes to them, would have the prohibited result, and
(b) the employer's sole or main purpose in making the offers is to achieve that result.

(2) The prohibited result is that the workers' terms of employment, or any of those terms, will not (or will no longer) be determined by collective agreement negotiated by or on behalf of the union.

(3) It is immaterial for the purposes of subsection (1) whether the offers are made to the workers simultaneously.

(5) A worker or former worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal on the ground that his employer has made him an offer in contravention of this section.

versions.description: Section 145B inserted by Employment Relations Act 2004 s.29, following the ECHR judgment in Wilson v United Kingdom [2002] which found that the UK's failure to protect against employer inducements to bypass collective bargaining violated Article 11 ECHR.

leadingCases.name: Kostal UK Ltd v Dunkley [2021] UKSC 47
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that an employer who made direct pay offers to employees during an ongoing collective bargaining process breached s.145B. The prohibited result was achieved because the offers, if accepted, would have determined the workers' pay otherwise than by collective agreement. The employer's purpose in making the offers was to achieve that result. Each offer to each worker triggered a separate mandatory award.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority confirming that direct offers to workers during collective bargaining can breach s.145B — potentially catastrophic financial exposure for employers with large unionised workforces

leadingCases.name: Wilson v United Kingdom [2002] ECHR 552
leadingCases.heldText: The ECHR held that the UK's failure to provide adequate protection against employer inducements to bypass collective bargaining violated Article 11 ECHR (freedom of association). This judgment directly led to the insertion of ss.145A–145F into TULRCA by the Employment Relations Act 2004.
leadingCases.significance: The ECHR judgment that triggered the creation of ss.145A–145F — directly protects the right to collective bargaining under Article 11

refs: s.145A TULRCA 1992 (inducements relating to union membership or activities)
s.145C–145F TULRCA 1992 (supplementary provisions on inducements)
ECHR Art.11 (freedom of association)
Employment Relations Act 2004 s.29 (insertion of ss.145A–145F)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/145B

sections.title: Detriment on grounds related to union membership or activities
sections.summary: A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment as an individual by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by the employer if the act or failure takes place for the sole or main purpose of preventing or deterring the worker from being or seeking to become a member of an independent trade union, from taking part in union activities at an appropriate time, or from making use of trade union services. A complaint may be presented to an employment tribunal. If the complaint is well-founded, the tribunal must make a declaration and may award compensation as it considers just and equitable.

versions.description: Employment Relations Act 2004 inserted subsection (1)(ba) — protection for making use of trade union services.

leadingCases.name: Fitzpatrick v British Railways Board [1992] ICR 221
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal considered the boundary between trade union 'activities' (protected) and individual conduct that merely happens to involve a union member (not protected). Activities of a trade union must be activities carried out in the capacity of a trade union member — they must have some connection with the union's collective functions.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the boundary between protected trade union activities and individual conduct

refs: s.152 TULRCA 1992 (dismissal on trade union grounds — automatically unfair)
s.148–151 TULRCA 1992 (supplementary provisions on detriment)
ECHR Art.11 (freedom of association)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/146

sections.title: Dismissal of employee on grounds related to union membership or activities
sections.summary: An employee's dismissal is automatically unfair if the reason or principal reason for the dismissal is that the employee was or proposed to become a member of an independent trade union, had taken part or proposed to take part in the activities of an independent trade union at an appropriate time, had made use of or proposed to make use of trade union services, or had failed to accept an offer made in contravention of s.145A or s.145B. No qualifying period of continuous employment is required. The normal cap on compensation does not apply — compensation is uncapped. Interim relief under s.161 is available — the employee can apply within 7 days of dismissal for an order continuing the contract pending full hearing.
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of Part X of the Employment Rights Act 1996 (unfair dismissal) the dismissal of an employee shall be regarded as unfair if the reason for it (or, if more than one, the principal reason) was that the employee—
(a) was, or proposed to become, a member of an independent trade union,
(b) had taken part, or proposed to take part, in the activities of an independent trade union at an appropriate time,
(ba) had made use, or proposed to make use, of trade union services at an appropriate time,
(bb) had failed to accept an offer made in contravention of section 145A or 145B, or
(c) was not a member of any trade union, or of a particular trade union, or of one of a number of particular trade unions, or had refused, or proposed to refuse, to become or remain a member.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision from Employment Acts 1980/1982.
Employment Relations Act 2004 inserted s.152(1)(ba) and (bb) — dismissal for failing to accept an offer in contravention of s.145A or s.145B is automatically unfair.

leadingCases.name: Fitzpatrick v British Railways Board [1992] ICR 221
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that participation in trade union activities 'at an appropriate time' means during working hours where the employer has consented, or outside working hours. Activities carried out without employer consent during working hours are not protected.
leadingCases.significance: Leading authority on the meaning of trade union activities 'at an appropriate time'.

refs: ERA 1996 s.108(3)(c) (no qualifying period for s.152 dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.124(1A) (no cap on compensation for s.152 dismissal)
s.161–166 TULRCA 1992 (interim relief — continuation of contract pending hearing)
s.146 TULRCA 1992 (detriment short of dismissal)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/152

sections.title: Protection from certain tort liabilities
sections.summary: An act done in contemplation or furtherance of a trade dispute is not actionable in tort on the ground only that it induces another person to break a contract or interferes with its performance, or that it consists in threatening that a contract will be broken or its performance interfered with, or that it is an interference with the trade, business or employment of another person. This is the statutory immunity — the foundation of lawful industrial action in the UK. The immunity only applies where the union has complied with all the statutory requirements (ballot, notice, etc.). If the requirements are not met, the employer can seek an injunction.
sections.text:
(1) An act done by a person in contemplation or furtherance of a trade dispute is not actionable in tort on the ground only that—
(a) it induces another person to break a contract or interferes or induces another person to interfere with its performance, or
(b) it consists in his threatening that a contract (whether one to which he is a party or not) will be broken or its performance interfered with, or that he will induce another person to break a contract or interfere with its performance.

(2) An agreement or combination by two or more persons to do or procure the doing of an act in contemplation or furtherance of a trade dispute is not actionable in tort if the act is one which if done without any such agreement or combination would not be actionable in tort.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision from TULRA 1974 s.13 (the 'golden formula' — acts done in contemplation or furtherance of a trade dispute).
Employment Relations Act 1999 amendments adjusted the scope of immunity.

leadingCases.name: Merkur Island Shipping Corporation v Laughton [1983] 2 AC 570
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords considered the scope of the statutory immunity in the context of secondary action. The immunity applies where the act is done 'in contemplation or furtherance of a trade dispute'. This requires a genuine trade dispute between workers and their employer.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the scope and limits of the statutory immunity for industrial action

refs: s.224 TULRCA 1992 (prohibition on secondary action — immunity does not apply)
s.226–235 TULRCA 1992 (ballot and notice requirements for immunity to apply)
s.244 TULRCA 1992 (meaning of 'trade dispute')
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/219

sections.title: Participation in official industrial action — unfair dismissal protection
sections.summary: An employee's dismissal is automatically unfair if the reason or principal reason for the dismissal is that the employee took protected industrial action. As amended by the Employment Rights Act 2025 (in force 18 February 2026), this protection is now unlimited in duration — the previous 12-week protected period has been removed. An employee who takes official industrial action that complies with all the statutory ballot and notice requirements cannot be fairly dismissed at any time for participating in that action. This is one of the most significant ERA 2025 changes for employers with unionised workforces.
sections.text:
(1) For the purposes of section 238A, an employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if—
(a) the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee took protected industrial action, and
(b) the dismissal took place.

[Note: As amended by ERA 2025 s.77, in force 18 February 2026, the previous subsection (3) which limited automatic unfair dismissal protection to the first 12 weeks of industrial action has been removed. Protection is now unlimited in duration.]

versions.description: Section 238A inserted by Employment Relations Act 1999 s.16. Originally provided 8 weeks' protected period.
Employment Relations Act 2004 extended protected period from 8 to 12 weeks.

Employment Rights Act 2025 s.77 removed the 12-week protected period entirely. Dismissal for protected industrial action is now automatically unfair at any time, regardless of the duration of the action. In force from 18 February 2026.
versions.policyRationale: The government's stated objective was to align the UK more closely with Article 11 ECHR (freedom of association) by removing the temporal limitation on dismissal protection during lawful industrial action.

leadingCases.name: Mercer v Alternative Future Group Ltd [2022] EWCA Civ 379
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that ERA 1996 s.148(1) (detriment for participation in industrial action) was incompatible with Article 11 ECHR. Workers subjected to detriment for participating in lawful industrial action could rely directly on Article 11.
leadingCases.significance: Extended ECHR Article 11 protection to detriment claims arising from industrial action participation. ERA 2025 subsequently strengthened these protections.

refs: ERA 2025 s.77 (removal of 12-week protected period — in force 18 February 2026)
s.219 TULRCA 1992 (statutory immunity — must be complied with for action to be 'protected')
s.226–235 TULRCA 1992 (ballot and notice requirements)
ECHR Art.11 (freedom of association — includes right to strike)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/238A

sections.title: ACAS codes of practice — issue by ACAS
sections.summary: ACAS may issue codes of practice containing practical guidance for the purpose of promoting the improvement of industrial relations, or for the purposes of promoting what appear to ACAS to be desirable practices in relation to the conduct of trade disputes. Before issuing a code, ACAS must consult organisations representative of employers, workers, and any other body it considers appropriate. A draft must be laid before Parliament and approved by resolution of each House. This is the enabling provision for all ACAS codes including the Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (Code 1), the Code on Settlement Agreements (Code 4), the Code on Flexible Working (Code 5), and the ACAS codes on disclosure of information and time off for trade union duties.
sections.text:
(1) ACAS may issue Codes of Practice containing such practical guidance as it thinks fit for the purpose of promoting the improvement of industrial relations.

(2) In particular, ACAS may, in accordance with this section, issue Codes of Practice containing practical guidance for the purpose of—
(a) promoting good practice in relation to the conduct of trade disputes, and
(b) promoting what appear to ACAS to be desirable practices in relation to any matter addressed by the codes.

(3) ACAS shall, before issuing a Code of Practice, consult such organisations of employers and workers and other bodies as it considers appropriate.

(4) When ACAS has prepared and published a draft of a proposed Code of Practice, it shall—
(a) consider any representations made to it about the draft, and
(b) if it thinks fit, modify the draft accordingly.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision. ACAS code-making power unchanged in substance.

refs: s.207A TULRCA 1992 (effect of failure to comply with Code — 25% adjustment)
s.201 TULRCA 1992 (codes of practice issued by Secretary of State)
ACAS Code of Practice 1 (Disciplinary and Grievance — acas-dg)
ACAS Code of Practice 4 (Settlement Agreements — acas-sa)
ACAS Code of Practice 5 (Flexible Working — acas-fw)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/199

sections.title: Effect of failure to comply with Code: adjustment of awards
sections.summary: If, in proceedings before an employment tribunal, any Code of Practice issued under s.199 appears to the tribunal to be relevant, the tribunal may, if it considers it just and equitable in all the circumstances, increase or decrease any award it makes by up to 25%. This applies where the employer or the employee has unreasonably failed to comply with any provision of a relevant Code. The 25% adjustment applies to the Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures, the Code on Settlement Agreements, and the Code on Flexible Working. It is the single most important enforcement mechanism for the ACAS codes — it provides a financial incentive for both employers and employees to follow the Code.
sections.text:
(1) This section applies to proceedings before an employment tribunal relating to a claim by an employee under any of the jurisdictions listed in Schedule A2.

(2) If, in the case of proceedings to which this section applies, it appears to the employment tribunal that—
(a) the claim to which the proceedings relate concerns a matter to which a relevant Code of Practice applies,
(b) the employer has failed to comply with that Code in relation to that matter, and
(c) that failure was unreasonable,
the employment tribunal may, if it considers it just and equitable in all the circumstances to do so, increase any award it makes to the employee by no more than 25%.

(3) If, in the case of proceedings to which this section applies, it appears to the employment tribunal that—
(a) the claim to which the proceedings relate concerns a matter to which a relevant Code of Practice applies,
(b) the employee has failed to comply with that Code in relation to that matter, and
(c) that failure was unreasonable,
the employment tribunal may, if it considers it just and equitable in all the circumstances to do so, reduce any award it makes to the employee by no more than 25%.

versions.description: Section 207A inserted by Employment Act 2008 s.3, replacing the previous statutory dispute resolution procedures with the Code-based approach. The 25% adjustment mechanism replaced the mandatory 3-step statutory procedures.

leadingCases.name: Slade v Biggs [2022] UKEAT/0149/21
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the application of the s.207A uplift and held that the tribunal must assess whether the failure to follow the Code was unreasonable in all the circumstances. A technical or minor non-compliance may not justify an adjustment, but a wholesale failure to follow the Code's requirements will typically result in an uplift at or near the 25% maximum.
leadingCases.significance: Recent authority on the application of the s.207A 25% adjustment — wholesale failure warrants maximum uplift

refs: s.199 TULRCA 1992 (ACAS codes of practice)
Schedule A2 TULRCA 1992 (list of jurisdictions to which s.207A applies)
Employment Act 2008 s.3 (insertion of s.207A)
ACAS Code of Practice 1 (acas-dg), Code 4 (acas-sa), Code 5 (acas-fw)
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1992/52/section/207A

sections.title: Conciliation
sections.summary: Where an employee has presented, or could present, a complaint to an employment tribunal, and a conciliation officer is requested to act or considers it could assist, the conciliation officer may endeavour to promote a settlement of the proceedings without their being determined by the tribunal. Anything communicated to a conciliation officer in connection with the performance of their functions is not admissible in evidence in any proceedings before a tribunal. This is the statutory foundation for ACAS conciliation — both voluntary conciliation and the mandatory Early Conciliation process introduced by ERA 1996 s.18A. Settlements reached through ACAS conciliation are recorded on a COT3 form and are binding.
sections.text:
(1) Where an employee has presented, or could present, a complaint to an employment tribunal, and a request is made to a conciliation officer (whether by that person or by the employer) to make his services available in the matter, the conciliation officer shall endeavour to promote a settlement of the proceedings without their being determined by an employment tribunal.

(2) Where an employee has presented a complaint to an employment tribunal, and a copy of the complaint has been sent to a conciliation officer, the conciliation officer shall endeavour to promote a settlement of the proceedings without their being determined by a tribunal.

(3) In exercising his functions under this section, a conciliation officer shall, where appropriate, have regard to the desirability of encouraging the use of other procedures available for the settlement of grievances.

(4) Anything communicated to a conciliation officer in connection with the performance of his functions under this section shall not be admissible in evidence in any proceedings before an employment tribunal, except with the consent of the person who communicated it to that officer.

versions.description: Original consolidated provision for ACAS conciliation.

ERA 1996 s.18A (inserted by Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7) introduced mandatory Early Conciliation. Since 6 May 2014, prospective claimants must contact ACAS before lodging a tribunal claim. The s.210 voluntary conciliation framework operates alongside the mandatory scheme.

leadingCases.name: Dunnachie v Kingston upon Hull City Council [2004] UKHL 36
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords considered the relationship between ACAS conciliation and tribunal proceedings. Anything communicated to an ACAS conciliation officer is inadmissible in tribunal proceedings (s.210(4)), providing a safe space for settlement negotiations.
leadingCases.significance: Confirms the confidentiality protection for ACAS conciliation communications.

parliamentaryOrigin.billTitle: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Bill [HL]

readings.keyPoints: The Lord Chancellor presented the Bill as the first consolidation of trade union and industrial relations law, bringing together legislation going back more than a century. Lord Wedderburn of Charlton (Labour) supported the consolidation, noting that the Bill also re-enacted provisions from the Employment Protection (Consolidation) Act 1978. The Bill was referred to the Joint Committee on Consolidation Bills in accordance with standard procedure.
readings.significance: As a consolidation Bill, TULRCA received minimal parliamentary debate on substance. The Joint Committee scrutinised the consolidation on behalf of Parliament. Lord Wedderburn noted the Bill was 'a first in another respect' — the first comprehensive consolidation of collective labour law.

readings.hansardRef: Joint Committee on Consolidation Bills, Session 1991-92
readings.keyPoints: The Joint Committee examined the Bill to verify it accurately consolidated existing legislation without making substantive changes. The consolidation brought together the Trade Union and Labour Relations Act 1974, Employment Acts 1980, 1982, 1988, and 1990, Trade Union Act 1984, and related provisions from the EPCA 1978.
readings.significance: The Joint Committee's approval confirmed the Bill properly consolidated the existing law. The scale of the consolidation was significant — it unified legislation spanning over a century of industrial relations reform.

readings.keyPoints: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 (c.52) received Royal Assent on 16 July 1992. The Act consolidated collective labour law into a single statute of 303 sections and multiple Schedules.
readings.significance: The largest consolidation of collective labour law in UK history. The Act has been extensively amended since — particularly by the Employment Relations Acts 1999 and 2004, the Trade Union Act 2016, and most recently the Employment Rights Act 2025.

committeeStage.note: Consolidation Bills do not receive substantive amendments. The Joint Committee verified that the Bill accurately consolidated existing law. Parliamentary Counsel drafted the Bill under the Law Commission's direction.

keyDebates.provision: General — Consolidation of collective labour law
keyDebates.ministerialStatement: The Lord Chancellor presented the Bill as consolidating enactments relating to trade unions, employers' associations, industrial relations and industrial action. He noted it was the first consolidation in this field, including legislation going back for more than a century. Lord Wedderburn noted the Bill would be the 'future reference point for debate about the law on industrial relations'.
keyDebates.significance: Established that TULRCA 1992 was purely a consolidation. All subsequent amendments (ERA 1999, ERA 2004, TUA 2016, ERA 2025) are amendments to the consolidated framework, not original policy.

precedingDocuments.title: Trade Union and Labour Relations Act 1974
precedingDocuments.significance: The original post-war framework for collective labour law, enacted by the Wilson Labour government. Established the statutory immunity for industrial action. Consolidated into TULRCA 1992.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Act 1980
precedingDocuments.significance: First Thatcher government restriction on industrial action — introduced secret ballots, restricted picketing, removed the statutory recognition procedure. Consolidated into TULRCA 1992.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Act 1982
precedingDocuments.significance: Removed trade union immunity from actions in tort, enabling unions to be sued for unlawful industrial action. Narrowed the definition of trade dispute. Consolidated into TULRCA 1992.

precedingDocuments.title: Trade Union Act 1984
precedingDocuments.significance: Required pre-strike ballots as a condition of the statutory immunity. Consolidated into TULRCA 1992 ss.226-235.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Acts 1988 and 1990
precedingDocuments.significance: Further restrictions on industrial action — prohibited secondary action (1990), gave individual union members rights against their unions (1988). Consolidated into TULRCA 1992.

explanatoryNotes.note: As a consolidation Act passed before the routine publication of Explanatory Notes (from 1998-99), TULRCA 1992 does not have official Explanatory Notes. The Second Reading Hansard debate and the Joint Committee report serve as the primary sources of parliamentary intent for the original consolidation. Subsequent amending Acts (ERA 1999, ERA 2004, TUA 2016, ERA 2025) have their own Explanatory Notes.

leadingCases.name: Kostal UK Ltd v Dunkley [2021] UKSC 47
leadingCases.heldText: Direct pay offers to workers during ongoing collective bargaining breached s.145B. Each offer to each worker triggers a separate mandatory fixed award.
leadingCases.significance: Potentially catastrophic financial exposure for employers who bypass collective bargaining.

leadingCases.name: Wilson v United Kingdom [2002] ECHR 552
leadingCases.heldText: The UK's failure to protect against employer inducements to bypass collective bargaining violated Article 11 ECHR.
leadingCases.significance: The ECHR judgment that triggered the creation of ss.145A-145F.

leadingCases.name: USDAW v Ethel Austin Ltd [2015] ICR 675
leadingCases.heldText: 'Establishment' in s.188(1) means the local employment unit, not the entire undertaking. ERA 2025 proposes to override this interpretation.
leadingCases.significance: The interpretation ERA 2025 will overturn — transformational for multi-site employers.

leadingCases.name: Susie Radin Ltd v GMB [2004] EWCA Civ 180
leadingCases.heldText: The starting point for the protective award is the maximum period; reduction only for mitigating circumstances. The award is penal, not compensatory.
leadingCases.significance: Maximum protective award is the starting point — doubled to 180 days by ERA 2025.

leadingCases.name: Junk v Kühnel [2005] ECJ Case C-188/03
leadingCases.heldText: Consultation must begin while proposals are at a formative stage, before final decisions are taken.
leadingCases.significance: Formative stage trigger for collective consultation.

leadingCases.name: Merkur Island Shipping v Laughton [1983] 2 AC 570
leadingCases.heldText: The statutory immunity requires a genuine trade dispute between workers and their employer.
leadingCases.significance: Scope and limits of the statutory immunity for industrial action.

leadingCases.name: Mercer v Alternative Future Group [2022] EWCA Civ 379
leadingCases.heldText: ERA 1996 s.148(1) was incompatible with Article 11 ECHR. Workers can rely directly on Article 11 for detriment arising from industrial action.
leadingCases.significance: Extended ECHR Article 11 protection. ERA 2025 subsequently strengthened these protections.

leadingCases.name: National Coal Board v NUM [1986] ICR 736
leadingCases.heldText: Collective agreements are not legally enforceable contracts absent express written provision — the presumption is conclusive.
leadingCases.significance: Foundational authority on non-enforceability of collective agreements.

leadingCases.name: Slade v Biggs [2022] UKEAT/0149/21
leadingCases.heldText: Wholesale failure to follow the ACAS Code warrants maximum 25% uplift under s.207A.
leadingCases.significance: Recent authority on maximum s.207A uplift application.

leadingCases.name: Keeley v Fosroc International [2006] EWCA Civ 1277
leadingCases.heldText: Collective agreement terms can be incorporated into individual contracts and become individually enforceable.
leadingCases.significance: Bridge between collective bargaining and individual contract rights.
